titleStmt.title: Kuwait 2022 – Speaker 13 – Conversation (Xrenig) 1.7 – Mother’s Secret Language
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Kuwait Fieldwork Campaign Gunda Kinzl 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: Yes, and there are some people, they don't like..., I have a friend, her name is Umm Ḥasan, when I tell her Umm Ḥaṣan she says "Please, call me Mimi don't call me Umm Ḥasan"
"Don't call me Umm Ḥasan, it makes me older" I said "What?" When I text her over Snapchat
"May God bless you ##" and things as such
She says "Please, Ṭifla, don't write that, write more what is in fashion
Her story is one worth knowing, she acts like a child, she acts like a child, she wants to be percieved young
I used to work in the department of family support in Fḥāḥīl around 12 years ago
My daughters would come from school to my work, I would go and pick them up and return to give my fingerprint (to clock in again)
And so my daughters, Reem and Hanān, would call me "Mummy, mummy", and there was a certain woman from the ʕAtāb tribe
She was a bit spoiled, even though she was my age
She would say "Ew! You let them call you mummy? If my daughter said that to me I would cut off her tongue"
She would say "No way!" even though she is in the exact same situation as I am, she is divorced and has only two daughters
/She mimicks the spoiled lady/ "If they called me mummy, I would kill them, that is what they should call my mother"
I said "What?!" The happiness that you feel when you hear "my mummy", you feel like I really did my job as a mother well
I didn't bring children only to dump them (on someone else), I brought them and raised them myself
And I gave my life to them, you /referring to the spoiled woman/, your goal was different, it was to dump them on your mother so that she raises them for you and you won't get attached to them
Get married to a ʕtēbi then, so that you can see a different life ((or get remarried)) ((not sure about translation##))
If my daughters didn't call me "mummy", then I would cut off their tongues
Even now, my daughters and sons call my mother "mummy", they don't call her "grandma"
This is sweet, but even if they called me "granny" I would like this, I like being referred to as a grandma, when...
What do they call you?
Me? They call me "mummy, mum", they refer to both of us as "mummy"
I heard that they use "yamma" for the grandmother, and "yumm" for the mother
Yes, yes, they actually say it as "yā"
This is pronounced by the Mahāmza, which is amongst our clan
I want to say something mum
I want to tell them now about the Mahāmza, I want to let them know about the Mahāmza
The Mahāmza do not say "yumma" like us, they say "yā", "yābā" (for the father)
This is a difference between the languages, we say "yūbā"
When I... at my workplace they laugh at me when I say "yūbā", they ask me "Are you from Iran?"
yūbā is Iranian? I said "Why?" (They said) "It's so cute when you say yūbā", I also say "yībē"
With the ʕAǧmāni dialect, it is "ib-i", "ix-i" (for the brother)
I said that's nice, ib-i, ix-i, umm-ič, imm-i, imm-i /she says all the ways that one can refer to family members with the ʕAǧmāni dialect/
Some of the tribes from the ʕAǧmān, because of my work, I have mixed with them a lot, I have mixed with the sedentary folk, I have mixed with the tribes, I have mixed with the Shi'ites
I have mixed... we are all slaves of God (we are all the same in the eyes of God)
With all the tribes, I am not young, I am 57, It's not little
Thank God, may God bless you
So, I have seen a lot, also I am one of those who loves the Arabic language and poetry, unlike my daughters
My daughters prefer the sciences
All my children took after their father, (they prefer) science, I always loved history, I love the language, I love the Arabic language, I love the fundamentals
I enjoy them, so all the stories, the poetry, I love writing, I love... this, everything that is in the Arabic language
I enjoy it, my teachers were Syrian
In the 1970s, my Arabic language teachers were Syrian
In Kuwait all the female teachers were Syrians and Palestinians
All of them, my history teacher was called Susan, up till now I remember her fondly and I still love her
I love her, and I loved their personalities, they had very strong personalities
Even though they were still young women, and they were beautiful
And I loved one of the teachers that my daughters had, Imān from Manṣūra
God bless, her eyes, do you see this colour? Golden
I used to stare at her, she was beautiful! Have you ever seen Barbie, the doll? (she looked like Barbie) She looked French
She looked French
She was very beautiful

With regards to us, the scale of beauty for the Bedouins, if we see a woman who is blonde and pale and her eyes are light, for example like you or you, "wow, amazing", for us this is the peak of beauty, because for us this is rare
Because this doesn't exist here
Yes, all the world like, first of all, the pale women
They like... but in the Arabic language the definition of beauty was not thinness and paleness
And you find people like us beautiful
They would say, she is almost # from the heaviness of her buttocks
### they liked this and they liked strength, they liked bigness, the Arabs of the past, not now
Before
Those who look like the Arabs
There are groups of people who look like Arabs, but they are not Arabis, they are mixed, they are mixed nowadays
The roots have mixed, their mothers are Filipinos, Indians, their mothers are Indians
A lot of roots have entered in Kuwait
You meet girls in school, and their mother is a Filipina
Can you count her as an Arab? She is mixed
The roots have been mixed with the Filipinos, with the Arabs, with the Syrians, with the Lebanese, however, if we go back to the Gulf and to the Arabs
We don't call it mixed roots
So if someone is married to someone from Saudi Arabia and such, it is not considered as mixed roots
No! Because they are Arabs, they are Arabs, the Filipinos aren't Arabs
Do you understand? The Indians aren't Arabs
And so this is mixed roots, it is not completely Arab, actually
However... they settled in Oman
They settled in Yemen, and they settled in some areas in the Gulf
And they... don't trace their roots back to Saudi Arabia
And Bahrain, most of them are from where? They are Baloch people from Iran
If you go and see how the language of Bahrain is, you will find that it is half Balochi, yes, half Balochi
And the United Arab Emirates? Where do the people come from? They can trace back to the Baloch people, and India and Pakistan
Their language, look I speak Balochi
Do you understand the speech of the Baloch? You don't understand it
Before we used to have a Persian language and we used to laugh at it, we would joke around with it when we were young
/speaker mimicks language/
/speaker mimicks language/
(It means) what I said to you, what you said to me, what he said to her
This was a language that was known by the generations of the 1960s, I learned it from my siblings
When we asked about it, they talked about it in a Kuwaiti series, they talked in it ##
We were taught it by the Ahwāz
Ahwāz people working for my father
It is a secret language amongst them, it has to do with the letters, the letters are changed and the word is pronounced with this changed letters
ši gayy igūl - this means what am I saying
ši gayy igāl-lah, and there is also a language called Tingara, they are two languages
/speaker mimicks language/
(This means) I will go to the market and spend all the money that I have
This is a secret language
It lies inbetween the Ahwāzi and the Balochi (languages)
And it was also in a Kuwaiti series, they speak about it and they laughed about it
Who knows it? We know it, mostly myself and my brother Falāḥ, and my sister Fatima
My brother Suʕūd, my mother and my father
From where did we learn it? From the workers in our fields
And now it's the Filipino language, our children go (like this) /speaker mimicks language/
What do you learn? Why? Because they are observing the servants that work for them
Our workers were different, they were Ahwāzi people and Iranians

Now when I speak, I can speak more than one language, why? I have practised with the Egyptians, I practised, the wife of my brother is Egyptian, her aunt is Egyptian (her refers to her daughter, Speaker13), the mother of her husband
My mother-in-law
I practised the Iraqi language, the wife of my maternal uncle, I used to listen to them when I was young, and children absorb, if they are smart, they absorb the language immediately
You would assume the language quite well, and we used to... if my father, God rest his soul, was with my mother
And we wanted to tell my mother a secret and we did not want our father to find out
You would say /speaker mimicks language/
/speaker mimicks language/ I would have told my mother, "Don't let my father know, tonight I will go to a wedding and return quickly"
Yes, do you see?
And it was..., if I recount this to my sister Fatima she laughs to this day
And so, I said to my sister Fatima why don't we teach it to our children?
So they would know the words and the origin of the letters, but she said "Who will teach them? Who knows this?"
